advaita.rendering: Where Krishna (yogeshvara, 'lord of all yogas,' their very source and seed) stands as the sovereign principle of all yoga, and where Partha (dhanur-dhara, 'bow-bearer') stands as his instrument of action, there abide shri (prosperity), vijaya (victory), bhuti (the fullness that is shri's expansion), and dhruva niti (the policy that does not waver). Shankara reads yatra-yatra ('where...where') as pakshe-pakshe ('in that camp, in that party') — this is a statement about alignment with the side where Ishvara himself operates. The verse closes the Gita not with metaphysics but with Sanjaya's empirical confidence: the outcome is assured precisely because jnana-shakti and karma-shakti are unified in one camp.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Shankara: 'yatra yogeshvarah sarva-yoganam ishvarah tat-prabhavatvat sarva-yoga-bijasya' — Krishna is yogeshvara because all yogas arise from him as seed; Partha is gandhiva-dhanva; shri, vijaya, bhuti (shri's expansion and spread), and dhruva niti (avyabhicharini) — all belong to that side.

vishishtadvaita.rendering: Where Krishna — lord of all the svabhava-yogas (the intrinsic natures) of every sentient and insentient being in their high and low states, whose very sankalpa (sovereign will) governs the form, existence, and activity-differences of all else — abides, and where Partha, son of his father's sister, the one whose feet alone are his sole refuge, stands as bow-bearer, there prosperity, victory, abundance, and unfailing right policy are immovably established. Ramanuja emphasizes the mutual relationship: Partha is tat-pada-dvandva-eka-ashraya, 'whose sole refuge is the pair of those feet,' making the verse a statement about the fruit of total surrender (prapatti) to Bhagavan.
vishishtadvaita.bhashya_anchor: Ramanuja: 'sva-sankalpa-ayatta-sva-itara-samasta-vastu-svarupa-sthiti-pravritti-bhedah krishno vasudeva-sunuh' and 'tat-pada-dvandva-eka-ashrayah' — the verse is about Partha as the archetype of the devotee whose sole shelter is the Lord's feet.

dvaita.rendering: The final verse seals the testimony that where the purna (complete, without defect) Mahavishnu is present and where his dependent devotee Partha stands as the instrument of his will, there all auspiciousness — shri, vijaya, bhuti, niti — is established with absolute certainty. For Madhva, the verse underlines the eternal, irrevocable ontological gap: jiva depends entirely on Hari; victory belongs to the side Hari graces, not because of Partha's excellence but because Vishnu's sovereignty is the only cause.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: Madhva's colophon verse: 'purnadoshamaha-vishnor gitam ashritya lesha-tah nirupanam krtam tena priyatam me sada vibhuh' — even this commentary is offered as service to Vishnu who is purnada-dosha-rahita (full, without flaw). The verse's certainty (dhruva) reflects Vishnu's invariant supremacy over all outcomes.
dvaita.flag: Madhva's direct bhashya on 18.78 is not separately preserved in the panel corpus; the colophon verses accompanying his Gita commentary have been used as the doctrinal anchor.

shuddhadvaita.rendering: Vallabha addresses Dhritarashtra directly: 'O King, having reflected on all this, be free of doubt — for consider: where Krishna the yogeshvara is, and where Partha the bow-bearer is, there alone are shri (royal-lakshmi), vijaya, and dhruva niti (certain, settled policy). Not elsewhere.' The certainty rests on Krishna's nature as Shripati ('lord of Shri') and Arjuna's as vijaya-svarupa ('very form of victory'): only their conjunction makes all outcomes firm. Vallabha's colophon makes the soteriological point explicit — until a person takes shelter in Krishna by the path taught here, moha (delusion) is not dissolved; but one who has done so attains Krishna's imperishable abode through pushti-bhakti.
shuddhadvaita.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha: 'bhagavatah shri-patitvat arjunasya vijayatvat tat-samyoge eva sarvam dhruvam nitish-cheti me matih' and 'yavatad-uktam-margena shri-krishnah sharanam mama / naro na bhavayet bhaktya tavan-moho na nashyati.'

bhakti.rendering: Sridhara reads the verse as Sanjaya's counsel to Dhritarashtra: 'Therefore, O King, abandon anxiety about the kingdom for your sons — for where Krishna the yogeshvara is present, and where Partha the Gandiva-bow-bearer is, there alone are shri (royal-lakshmi), vijaya, bhuti (ever-increasing prosperity), and dhruva niti (certain policy) — this is my conviction.' The theological summary follows: bhagavad-bhakti-yuktasya tat-prasadatma-bodhatah — for one possessed of devotion to Bhagavan, through the grace that arises from that devotion comes self-knowledge, and from that, liberation (bandha-vimuktih). Bhakti is the sole efficient cause of moksha; jnana functions only as its subordinate instrument.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Sridhara: 'bhagavad-bhakti-yuktasya tat-prasadatma-bodhatah sukham bandha-vimuktih syad iti gitartha-sangrahah' — his verse summary of the entire Gita.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Madhusudana reads yogeshvara as 'sarva-yoga-siddhinam ishvarah sarva-jnah sarva-shaktih bhagavan krishnah bhakta-duhkha-karshanas' — the omniscient, omnipotent Bhagavan who draws away the sorrows of his devotees — and pairs him with Arjuna as 'nara,' such that the Yudhishthira-paksha is the site of Nara-Narayana jointly. Where they stand together, shri (royal-lakshmi), vijaya (enemy-defeating excellence), and bhuti (ever-increasing royal-lakshmi) are avashyam-bhavinI (inevitably coming). The purpose is practical counsel: Dhritarashtra should abandon futile hope in his son's victory and seek reconciliation with the Pandavas, who are graced by Lakshmi, victory, and the Lord alike.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusudana: 'nara-narayana-adhisthite tasmin yudhishthira-pakshe shri raja-lakshmih vijayah shatru-parajaya-nimitta utkarsho bhutir uttarottaram raja-lakshmya vivrddhi dhruva avashyam-bhavini' and his closing verse: 'krishnat param kim api tattvam aham na jane.'

so_what_questions: Sanjaya has been a mere reporter throughout; why does the Gita end with his personal conviction (matir mama) rather than with Krishna or Arjuna speaking — what does it mean that the testimony of an unbiased witness closes the text?

The verse pairs two presences — the divine (yogeshvara Krishna) and the human (Partha dhanur-dhara) — as jointly necessary for any of the four fruits. What does it say about the structure of right action that neither alone suffices?
The four terms — shri, vijaya, bhuti, niti — span material prosperity, outward success, expanding abundance, and right governance. Why does the Gita close with worldly fruits rather than moksha?
Dhruva means 'fixed, immovable, like the pole star.' Why is niti (policy / right conduct) the thing that is specifically called dhruva, rather than victory or prosperity?

Each of the six schools reads the verse through its own soteriology — Shankara sees it as empirical confirmation of jnana-shakti + karma-shakti alignment; Ramanuja sees it as the fruit of prapatti; Madhva sees it as Vishnu's invariant sovereignty; Vallabha sees it as the fruit of pushti-bhakti. Whose reading does the verse's grammar most directly support, and why?
Sanjaya is narrating to Dhritarashtra, whose son is about to die. Why is this benediction — assuring the opposing side of victory — the last thing Sanjaya says to him?

everyday_applications.advaita: Before entering any high-stakes endeavor, ask: is the source of guidance in this situation aligned with viveka (discernment) rather than ego-agenda? Shankara's reading says the outcome is determined by which 'side' Ishvara's seed-power operates through — in practice, align with clarity rather than with personal gain, and treat the result as already settled.
everyday_applications.vishishtadvaita: In moments of total helplessness — job loss, illness, relational rupture — Ramanuja's Partha is the archetype: the one whose only refuge is the Lord's feet, with no backup plan. The everyday application is tat-pada-dvandva-eka-ashraya: let one thing be your unconditional shelter and act from that shelter rather than from calculated self-protection.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's reading is a corrective to pride in one's own competence: the dhanur-dhara (bow-bearer) is not the cause of victory; Vishnu's sovereign grace is. In any domain where you have genuine skill, the daily practice is to exercise that skill fully while inwardly attributing the outcome to the source that gave you the skill in the first place.
everyday_applications.shuddhadvaita: Vallabha addresses Dhritarashtra — someone clinging to false hope. The everyday application: identify the one thing you are irrationally insisting must go your way (a relationship, a business outcome, a child's life path), and ask whether that insistence is grounded in where Krishna-and-Partha are, or merely in where your desire is. Pushti-bhakti means releasing that insistence into prasada.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's summary — bhakti as the sole efficient cause, jnana as its instrument — has a practical implication: invest in the quality of your devotion (love, attention, consistency) rather than in accumulated knowledge or technique. Knowledge that does not issue from love is like firewood without flame; bhakti is the flame.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's Nara-Narayana pairing suggests that every important human endeavor needs both a divine anchor (the value or purpose that transcends the project) and a skilled human instrument. In team or organizational life: before major decisions, ask whether both are present — a clear, transcendent purpose and a person of genuine capability acting from that purpose. The absence of either makes dhruva niti impossible.

self_assessment: All six schools anchored in supplied bhashya prose with genuine doctrinal divergence; Madhva flag included for absent direct-bhashya; no smart quotes, no unescaped double quotes, valid JSON.
